handshape: Bring your fingertips together in front of you a couple of times, as if pinching a little 'more'.
steps: Bunch the fingertips of each hand together into two little points.
Tap the two points together in front of you.
Repeat the tap a couple of times.
when: When your child wants more food, play, or another turn.

handshape: Bring a flat hand to your mouth as if putting food in.
steps: Hold one hand flat with fingers together.
Bring your fingertips up to your mouth, as if putting food in.
Tap lightly a couple of times.
when: At mealtimes, or when offering a snack.

handshape: Make a 'C' shape with your hand and tip it towards your mouth like a cup.
steps: Curve your hand into a 'C' shape, like holding a cup.
Bring it up to your mouth.
Tip it gently towards you as if taking a sip.
when: When offering a drink or your child is thirsty.

handshape: Hold both flat hands up, then turn them over and out, like 'all done'.
steps: Hold both flat hands up, palms facing you.
Turn them over so the palms face out.
Flick them out to the sides, like 'all done'.
when: When an activity, meal, or play is over.

handshape: Rest one fist on your open flat palm and lift both together.
steps: Make a fist with one hand.
Rest the fist on your other open, flat palm.
Lift both hands up together.
when: When your child needs a hand — a powerful early word.

handshape: Rub your flat hand in a small circle on your chest.
steps: Place your flat hand on the middle of your chest.
Rub it in a small circle.
Keep it gentle and smooth.
when: To model polite requests alongside speech.

handshape: Curve one hand and tap its fingertips into your other open palm.
steps: Curve one hand into a shallow scoop.
Hold your other hand open and flat.
Tap the curved fingertips into the open palm.
when: For another turn — great during play and songs.

handshape: Bring the side of one flat hand down onto your other open palm.
steps: Hold one hand open and flat, palm up.
Hold your other hand flat, like a blade.
Bring the blade hand down sharply onto the open palm.
when: To pause an activity, or for safety.

handshape: Make a fist and nod it up and down, like a nodding head.
steps: Make a fist with one hand.
Bend it up and down at the wrist.
Think of a little head nodding 'yes'.
when: Answering and choosing — pair with a nod.

handshape: Tap your thumb and first two fingers together, like a closing beak.
steps: Hold out your thumb and first two fingers.
Tap them together, like a beak closing.
Do it a couple of times.
when: Answering and refusing — pair with a head shake.

handshape: Tap your thumb on the side of your chin a couple of times.
steps: Spread your hand with the thumb sticking out.
Tap your thumb on the side of your chin.
Tap a couple of times.
when: Naming a parent — motivating and very early.

handshape: Tap your thumb on your forehead a couple of times.
steps: Spread your hand with the thumb sticking out.
Tap your thumb on your forehead.
Tap a couple of times.
when: Naming a parent — motivating and very early.

handshape: Pat the side of your leg, as if calling a dog.
steps: Hold your hand flat by your side.
Pat the side of your leg a couple of times.
Think of calling a dog over.
when: Animals are motivating early words.

handshape: Curve both hands as if holding a round ball.
steps: Curve both hands as if cupping a round ball.
Bring your fingertips toward each other.
Hold the round shape for a moment.
when: A favourite toy and an easy first word.

handshape: Rest your cheek on your flat hand, like sleeping.
steps: Hold one hand flat.
Tilt your head to the side.
Rest your cheek on your hand, like sleeping.
when: Bedtime and nap routines.

handshape: Rub both fists up and down on your chest, like washing.
steps: Make a fist with each hand.
Place them on your chest.
Rub them up and down, like washing.
when: Bath-time routine.

handshape: Hold both open hands out in front, palms up, and draw them gently towards you.
steps: Hold both hands out in front, palms facing up.
Curl your fingers slightly.
Draw your hands in towards your body, like 'gimme'.
when: When your child is asking for an object or activity.

handshape: Point two fingers from your eyes out towards the thing you're looking at.
steps: Hold up your first two fingers in a 'V'.
Start with them pointing back near your eyes.
Turn them out towards what you want to look at.
when: To share attention — point out something interesting together.

handshape: Hold one flat hand up, palm out, and hold it still for a moment.
steps: Hold one hand up flat, palm facing out.
Keep it still and steady.
Pair it with a calm 'wait' so your child learns to pause.
when: To help your child pause and take turns.

handshape: Raise an open hand and give a friendly wave.
steps: Raise one open hand up beside your head.
Wave it gently from side to side.
Smile and say 'hello' as you wave.
when: Greeting people — a warm, very early social word.

handshape: Touch your fingertips to your chin, then bring your hand forward and down.
steps: Touch the fingertips of a flat hand to your chin.
Bring your hand forward and down, away from your face.
Pair it with a warm 'thank you'.
when: Modelling polite, grateful words alongside speech.

handshape: Hold both flat hands together, then open them like opening a book.
steps: Press both flat hands together, palms touching.
Keep the little-finger sides together.
Open your hands like opening a book.
when: Story time and shared reading.

handshape: Hold both hands as if on a steering wheel and turn it side to side.
steps: Hold both loose fists out in front, like gripping a wheel.
Turn them as if steering.
Add a 'brrm' sound for fun.
when: Going out, or playing with toy cars.

handshape: Make a fist with your thumb poking out between two fingers and give it a little shake.
steps: Make a fist with one hand.
Poke your thumb out between your first two fingers.
Give it a small shake from side to side.
when: Toileting routines — a useful everyday word.
